name: BD Alm
classification_notes: The letterforms are inspired by Katakana characters from a Japanese pharmacy sign, giving the Latin alphabet a stylised, angular quality more suited to display use than running text.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the Katakana lettering on a Tokyo pharmacy sign, applying that angular aesthetic to the Latin alphabet.
description: Created during a stay in Tokyo in 2001, BD Alm translates the visual character of Katakana script into Latin letterforms. The result is a single-weight display face with a distinctly angular, stylised quality suited to headlines and short text rather than body copy. Coverage is limited to Basic Latin and a small selection of Latin-1 Supplement glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Alm as a font created during a stay in Tokyo, Japan, inspired by Katakana characters on a pharmacy shop sign. It is offered as a free download for personal projects, with a commercial licence available for purchase.

name: BD Algebra
classification_notes: The typeface is explicitly described as a geometric grotesk, combining the circular, constructed forms of geometric sans-serifs with the workhorse character of a grotesque.
summary: A single-weight geometric grotesque released in 2009 as a free personal-use font, with commercial licensing available.
description: Available in two subfamilies, BD Algebra Plus and BD Algebra Minus, the typeface covers Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and a modest selection of Latin Extended-A characters, supporting Western European languages including Danish, French, German and Spanish. It is a straightforward geometric grotesque with no italic or condensed cuts. Free for personal projects; a commercial licence must be purchased separately.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Algebra as a geometric grotesk designed in 2009, offered as a free download for personal use with a paid licence required for commercial projects.

name: BD Alien
classification_notes: BD Alien is a display typeface with an explicitly electronic, sci-fi aesthetic, designed for expressive use rather than body text. Its variable width and weight axes, along with its described 'blips and bleeps' character, place it firmly in the techno display subcategory.
summary: A variable sci-fi display typeface with weight and width axes spanning Regular to Black and wide to slim cuts.
description: Designed for science fiction and electronic contexts, BD Alien is available as both static and variable font files, with the variable version covering weight from Regular to Black and width from wide to slim. The family ships in six static styles alongside four VF cuts, covering Latin script across 25 European languages. It suits poster work, game interfaces and any brief calling for a retro-futurist or outer-space tone.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Alien as an electronic typeface from outer techno space, offering blips and bleeps in regular, bold and black from wide to slim. They position it as created for the next sci-fi generation, asking playfully: 'Is there anybody out there?'

name: BD AsciiMax
classification_notes: A display typeface that simulates ASCII art typography, where letterforms are constructed from text characters to mimic the visual output of BBS and Usenet systems. This places it firmly in the display category as a novelty or period-style face rather than a functional text or mono font.
summary: A single-weight display font that recreates the ASCII art lettering style of late-1970s and early-1980s bulletin board systems.
description: Each letterform is built from text characters arranged to simulate the kind of logotypes and titles that BBS and Usenet users assembled within the constraints of early computer hardware. Released in 1999, the face covers Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and a handful of additional glyphs including Greek and currency symbols. Its appeal is primarily archival and nostalgic, suited to retro computing aesthetics and period-appropriate branding.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD AsciiMax as a font that simulates the ASCII typography style found on Usenet news messages and computer bulletin board systems of the late 1970s and early 1980s, when the limitations of hardware required text characters to be used in place of images, logotypes and titles.

name: BD Aubergin
classification_notes: A geometric display typeface with Bauhaus-influenced construction and a distinctive 'sliced' decorative treatment, positioning it firmly in the display category rather than a functional geometric sans.
summary: A Bauhaus-inflected display face with a variable axis that morphs letterforms from solid to sliced, suited to titling and poster work.
description: Released in 2020 by Type Different, BD Aubergin draws on geometric, Bauhaus-era construction while adding a distinctive 'slice' motif to its letterforms. The variable version provides an axis running from a filled, flat state to the sliced treatment, giving designers control over how expressive the type reads. Language support spans broad Western and Central European Latin, covering 26 languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Aubergin as 'the typeface with a slice', noting its Bauhaus-era reminiscence with a modern twist and recommending it for titling and adding character to both print and screen work. The variable version morphs from filled (flat) to sliced across its axis.

name: BD Barbeaux
classification_notes: A condensed display typeface with Art Nouveau influences, designed for fashion headlines rather than extended text. The Numérique variant adds rounded corners, reinforcing its decorative display character.
summary: A condensed Art Nouveau display face aimed at fashion and lifestyle headlines, offered in a standard cut and a rounded Numérique variant.
description: BD Barbeaux is a condensed typeface with a chic, Art Nouveau character suited to fashion editorials and lifestyle publications. Two variants are available: the standard Regular and BD Barbeaux Numérique, which softens the design with rounded corners. Latin script support covers a broad range of Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Barbeaux as a condensed typeface that is chic and fashionable with a touch of Art Nouveau, released in 2015. The Numérique version features rounded corners, and the family is pitched as ideal for fashion and lifestyle headlines in blogs and magazines.

name: BD Bermuda
classification_notes: An uppercase-only geometric serif designed for titling, drawing on negative space and minimalist form rather than text use; best classified as display due to its decorative, fashion-oriented intent.
summary: An uppercase geometric serif from 2007 built around negative space and clean forms, aimed at fashion and cosmetics titling.
description: BD Bermuda is a single-weight, uppercase geometric serif conceived for headline use in fashion and cosmetics contexts. Its design plays with the strategic removal of shape to create an elegant, restrained aesthetic. Coverage spans Latin Extended-A, supporting a broad range of Western and Central European languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Bermuda as a geometric serif uppercase alphabet that plays with the absence of shapes and the elegance of the white room, positioning it as a typeface suited to fashion and cosmetics titling work.

name: BD BerneBeats
classification_notes: A round, bold, single-weight display face designed explicitly for festival logotypes and titling, with no text utility intended. Its chunky, rhythmic letterforms place it firmly in decorative display territory.
summary: A single-weight round display face from Büro Destruct, aimed squarely at festival logotypes and music titling work.
description: Released by Büro Destruct in 2004, BD BerneBeats is a chunky, round typeface built for headline and logotype use at large sizes. The letterforms carry a bold, rhythmic quality suited to pop music and festival contexts. Coverage extends across Latin Western European languages including Swiss-German, French, German and Scandinavian scripts.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD BerneBeats as the perfect pop music festival logotype and titling font: round, bold and catchy, with 100% groove from Büro Destruct's Berne Capital studio.

name: BD Berlin
classification_notes: An all-caps geometric display typeface described as bold and rock-solid, with alternate lowercase characters. Its blocky, wall-like construction places it firmly in the display category rather than a functional text face.
summary: A single-weight all-caps display face designed during a stay in Berlin, offering alternate lowercase glyphs and a heavy, blocky character.
description: Bold and blocky, BD Berlin (also released as BD Pankow) was drawn during an extended stay in Berlin and carries that city's blunt, monumental energy. It operates as an uppercase typeface with alternates mapped to the lowercase slots, giving designers some variation within a single style. Coverage spans a solid range of Latin-Extended languages including German, French, Spanish and Scandinavian scripts.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Berlin as bold and rock-solid, designed during an extended stay in Berlin. Also known as BD Pankow, it is an uppercase font with alternate characters on the lowercase positions, summed up by the foundry as "another brick in the wall".

name: BD Cardhole
classification_notes: An all-caps display face with deliberately distressed, creepy letterforms designed for decorative horror-themed use rather than body text.
summary: A single-weight free display face built around deliberately unsettling letterforms suited to horror or lo-fi punk aesthetics.
description: BD Cardhole offers one weight of upper and lowercase letters, both described by the foundry as intentionally horrifying in character. The glyph set is minimal, covering Basic Latin with a handful of punctuation and alternates. It is a free download aimed squarely at decorative horror or rebellious graphic contexts rather than any serious typographic application.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Cardhole as a two-style uppercase creepy free font released in 2021, positioning it as ideal for a next terrible revolt, with Basic Latin language support.

name: BD Brick
classification_notes: A single-weight display face with a modular, block-like construction originating from club culture graphic design, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric character.
summary: A single-weight display face born from 1996 Swiss club graphics, built on modular brick-like letterforms and updated twice since its original release.
description: Originally drawn for a Saturday night house music event in Biel/Bienne, Switzerland, BD Brick carries the visual energy of 1990s club culture in its tight, block-constructed letterforms. The typeface received updates in 2007 and 2022, broadening its character set to cover Latin Extended languages across Europe and Africa. With only one style available, it suits headline and logotype work where a chunky, period-specific geometry is the point.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Brick as a typeface originally created in 1996 for a regular Saturday night house music event called Cream at Take5 in Biel/Bienne, Switzerland, with subsequent updates in 2007 and 2022.

name: BD Caramel
classification_notes: BD Caramel is a variable display typeface with fluid, liquid-like letterforms intended for titling use, making it a decorative display type rather than a conventional script or serif.
summary: A variable display typeface reworking the 2014 BD Qualle, with a variable axis that animates letterforms into fluid, liquid-like shapes suited to food and cookery contexts.
description: Released in 2023, BD Caramel is a variable remake of BD Qualle, offering two named styles, Regular and Bubble, with a variable axis that shifts letterforms between them. The fluid, rounded character of the type makes it well suited to titling in cookbooks, packaging or any context where a liquid or confectionery mood is required. Latin coverage spans Western and Northern European languages including French, German, Dutch and the Scandinavian languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Caramel as a variable typeface remake of their 2014 BD Qualle font, noting that the variable feature makes the letters dance on the milky-way and that it is perfect for titlings in cookbooks or anything liquid related.

name: BD Colonius
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as circular and geometric, placing it firmly in the geometric sans-serif tradition with an emphasis on round, optically even letterforms.
summary: A circular geometric sans-serif from 2001, available in a single Regular weight with a refined, almost precious character.
description: Built around circular forms and geometric construction, BD Colonius sits in the tradition of round geometric sans-serifs but with a restrained elegance rather than the neutrality of more utilitarian designs. It covers a broad range of Latin-script languages including most Western and Northern European languages, with OpenType support for standard ligatures and tabular figures. At a single weight it is suited to display and branding contexts where purity of form matters more than typographic range.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Colonius as an elegant, circular, geometric sans-serif typeface released in 2001, evoking the character of a fine perfume, with the Victor Hugo quote 'Nothing awakens a memory like a smell' used to set its mood.

name: BD Cashbox
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface modelled on cash slip receipt printing, with additional styles referencing dot-matrix and typewriter output. Clearly a fixed-width display/novelty mono.
summary: A monospaced typeface modelled on cash register receipt printing, originally created in 2001 and expanded in 2022 with dot-matrix and typewriter-style variants.
description: Designed originally for a book on money and value, this fixed-width face mimics the output of thermal receipt printers. The 2022 update broadened the glyph set and introduced two additional styles: DotMatrix, referencing needle-print output, and Letter, suggesting typewriter impression. Coverage spans over twenty Latin-based languages across Western and Northern Europe.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Cashbox as a monospaced typeface in the style of cash slip receipts, originally designed in 2001 for a publication on the theme of money and value. In 2022 the font was expanded with additional characters and two new styles, DotMatrix and Letter.

name: BD Central
classification_notes: A bold, uppercase-only alphabet designed for display use, with alternative capitals on lowercase keys; its single-weight, all-caps nature places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A bold, uppercase-only display face originally drawn in 2002, with alternative capitals mapped to lowercase keys and broad Latin language coverage added in 2023.
description: Originally designed in 2002 and updated in 2023 with expanded language support, BD Central offers a single Regular weight comprising bold uppercase letterforms throughout. Alternative capital variants are accessible via the lowercase keys, giving some compositional flexibility within the all-caps constraint. The broad Latin coverage spans over 25 languages including Czech, Polish, Turkish and Icelandic.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Central as a bold uppercase-only alphabet with alternative capital letters on the lowercase keys, originally designed in 2002 and updated in 2023 with language support and optimisation.

name: BD Console Remix
classification_notes: A remixed console-style typeface with a pixel-derived aesthetic rooted in late-1990s Japanese digital design culture, placing it firmly in the display category with a retro-tech subcategory.
summary: A late-1990s pixel-style remix originally produced by Savwo at Cyclone Graphix in Tokyo, available in Regular and Italic.
description: Remixed in 1998 by Savwo from Cyclone Graphix, Tokyo, this console-flavoured display face carries the chunky, screen-grid aesthetic common to Japanese digital design of that era. The family is minimal: Regular and Italic only, with Basic Latin coverage and a selection of Latin-1 Supplement characters. It suits retro game interfaces, lo-fi editorial work and any project leaning into late-1990s digital nostalgia.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Console Remix as a remixed font version by Savwo from Cyclone Graphix, Tokyo, produced in 1998. They also point visitors to the original variable version of BD Console.

name: BD Cyborg
classification_notes: A stylised display typeface designed explicitly for science-fiction contexts, with angular and futuristic letterforms rather than any conventional text classification.
summary: An updated version of the 2004 BD Deckard font, reworked in 2023 with broader language support for science-fiction design work.
description: Originally released as BD Deckard in 2004, this single-weight display face was rebuilt in 2023 with expanded Latin language coverage including Danish, Dutch, French, German and fourteen others. The letterforms carry the angular, futuristic quality suited to sci-fi editorial, game titles and thematic branding. Available in one style at a single price point.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Cyborg as an updated version of their BD Deckard font from 2004, featuring wider language support and other optimisations, positioned as ideal for any sci-fi related project.

name: BD Console
classification_notes: BD Console is a pixel-style electronic display font with a deliberately lo-fi, digital aesthetic, placing it firmly in the display category with a pixel or electronic subcategory.
summary: A pixel-style electronic display font originally from 1997, reissued as a variable font with broader Latin language support.
description: Originally designed in 1997, BD Console draws on the visual language of electronic displays and early digital screens. The 2023 update introduces a variable font version alongside Regular and Game cuts, adding wider glyph coverage across Western European Latin languages. Best suited to gaming, retro-themed design and anything that calls for an unabashedly digital character.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Console as a funky electronic font first designed in 1997, now updated to a variable font with wider language support and more glyphs. They also offer a Remix version, and sign off with the line 'The electric sun always shines on TV.'

name: BD Eject
classification_notes: BD Eject is a stylised Y2K-era mixed-case display typeface with a distinctly decorative, culture-specific aesthetic that places it firmly in the display category rather than any neutral or text-oriented style.
summary: A Y2K mixed-case display typeface from 1998, later remixed with Japanese Katakana variants by Savwo of Cyclone Graphix, Tokyo.
description: Originally created in 1998 and delivered on floppy disk, BD Eject carries an authentic late-nineties digital aesthetic in its mixed-case forms. Savwo from Cyclone Graphix in Tokyo later extended the family with Katakana variants in three flavours: Lowerphat, Upperphat and Regular. The family covers a broad range of Latin languages alongside Japanese Katakana, making it a distinctive choice for Y2K-inflected design work with cross-cultural reach.
foundry_blurb: BD Eject is described as a phat Y2K mixed-case typeface created in 1998, with Savwo from Cyclone Graphix, Tokyo remixing the original design to include Japanese Katakana glyphs across Lowerphat, Upperphat and Regular flavours.

name: BD Discount
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric grotesk sans serif, with circular constructions and a logotype-first design ethos that places it firmly in the geometric grotesque tradition.
summary: A tightly geometric grotesque originally drawn for the Büro Discount logotype in 2003, updated in 2023 with improved kerning, expanded glyphs and stylistic alternates.
description: Born from a 2003 logotype commission, BD Discount is a geometric sans with a clean, constructed character suited to logotyping and titling work. Its 2023 revision added two stylistic sets with alternate letterforms, better kerning and a broader glyph set, whilst keeping the family to two weights: Regular and Bold. Latin script coverage spans Western European languages including French, German, Portuguese and Swedish.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Discount as a very geometric grotesk sans serif, originally designed in 2003 alongside the Büro Discount logotype. Updated in 2023, it now features two weights, improved kerning, more glyphs and alternate glyphs via stylistic sets, making it particularly suited to logotyping and titling.

name: BD Doomed
classification_notes: A squarish, all-caps-styled display typeface with a pixel-influenced geometric construction, typical of late 1990s techno/digital display fonts. The name 'Squareup' underlines its angular, grid-based letterforms.
summary: A squarish, techno-inflected display face from 1998, revived in 2024 with expanded Latin language support.
description: Originally designed in 1998, BD Doomed Squareup carries the angular, grid-influenced aesthetic of late-nineties digital typography. The 2024 update extended its character set to cover Danish, Dutch, French, German, Icelandic, Indonesian and a range of other European languages. It remains a single-style typeface suited to display settings where its rigid geometry reads as a deliberate stylistic choice.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Doomed Squareup as a typeface designed in 1998 that received a 2024 update bringing much wider language support, noting that 'this font is not doomed anymore.'

name: BD ElMax
classification_notes: A single-weight display typeface derived from the Maxell audio cassette tape logotype, placing it firmly in the retro display category with a strong industrial and brand-revival character.
summary: A single-weight display typeface drawn from the Maxell audio cassette tape logotype, designed by BD intern Caspar Herselman in 2003.
description: Originally created in 2003 by Type Different intern Caspar Herselman, BD ElMax lifts its letterforms directly from the Maxell cassette tape brand identity. It exists as a single Regular weight covering Western European Latin, with modest extended language support added in 2023. Best suited to retro-themed design work, packaging and editorial contexts where a mid-century analogue aesthetic is intended.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD ElMax as a typeface inspired by the Maxell audio cassette tape logotype, designed by BD intern Caspar Herselman in 2003, with slightly extended language support added in 2023.

name: BD Electrobazar 2008
classification_notes: A club-culture display face with angular, mechanical letterforms designed for rave and techno aesthetics. The all-caps character and event-poster heritage place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A rave-culture display face from 1995, updated in 2008, originally distributed by Büro Destruct on floppy disk for the European club scene.
description: Originally created in 1995 as part of the Büro Destruct catalogue and sold commercially by Apply Design Group in Germany, the typeface found its footing on event flyers, posters and album covers tied to the techno and rave circuit. The 2008 update expanded the glyph set while preserving the angular, hard-edged character of the original. Available in Regular and Italic, it covers a broad range of Latin-based European languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Electrobazar as speaking the language of rave parties, techno-house music and clubbing, noting its widespread use on event flyers, posters and album covers. Originally one of the Büro Destruct fonts from 1995, it was distributed commercially on floppy disk by Apply Design Group in Germany before receiving a 2008 update with an expanded glyph set.

name: BD Elk
classification_notes: BD Elk renders Latin input as Nordic runic script forms, making it a display typeface used for its symbolic visual character rather than conventional legibility. The foundry classifies it under Dingbats.
summary: A single-weight typeface that maps Latin characters to Nordic runic forms, originally drawn in 1997 and compiled into a font in 2012.
description: Each keystroke outputs a runic glyph, allowing users to type in a Nordic runic script without needing a decoding reference. The shapes were first created in 1997 and eventually formalised into a typeface in 2012. Available free for personal use, with a commercial licence available for purchase.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Elk as a way to type Nordic runic script without decoding instructions, with the underlying glyph shapes originally created in 1997 and combined into a typeface in 2012. It is offered as a free font for personal projects, with a commercial licence available.

name: BD EmeraldMineColor
classification_notes: A colour font revival of bitmap lettering from an Amiga game, placing it firmly in the display/pixel category with no serif or sans-serif structural logic.
summary: A colour font recreation of the bitmap lettering from the 1987 Amiga game Emerald Mine, made available as a free download.
description: Based on the typeface used in Emerald Mine, the Kingsoft boulder-dash game released for the Amiga in 1987, this revival was packaged as a colour font in 2020. Coverage is limited to basic Latin characters, punctuation and a handful of currency symbols, reflecting the minimal glyph set of the original game font. Best suited to retro gaming projects, fan work or anything that wants an authentic slice of late-1980s microcomputer aesthetics.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD EmeraldMineColor as a colour font featuring the typeface used in the Amiga game Emerald Mine, published by Kingsoft in Germany in 1987 and designed by Klaus Heinz and Volker Wertich. It is available as a free download and was released in 2020.

name: BD Fazer
classification_notes: A slanted, lowercase-only racing-themed display face with an aggressive italic posture; designed explicitly for sporty, high-speed visual contexts rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight slanted display face from 1996, designed around a racing aesthetic with a lowercase-only character set.
description: Originally drawn in 1996 and updated in 2023 with improved kerning and broader language coverage, BD Fazer is a leaning, speed-oriented display font aimed squarely at motorsport and action contexts. It offers only a single weight and no upright or italic variants beyond its inherent slant. Useful for titles and short-burst copy where an aggressive sporting tone is required.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Fazer as a speedy slanted lowercase-only font with a sportive, racing character, originally released in 1996 and refreshed in 2023 with wider language support and better kerning. They sum it up simply as 'Ready Set Go.'

name: BD Exercise
classification_notes: BD Exercise is a dingbat or pictographic font consisting entirely of exercise illustrations mapped to alphabetic characters rather than conventional letterforms, placing it firmly in the display/symbol category.
summary: A dingbat font of 26 exercise pictographs mapped to alphabetic keys, available in light and bold weights across lowercase and uppercase respectively.
description: Each of the 26 characters maps to a different exercise illustration, with light-weight figures assigned to lowercase letters and bold-weight figures to capitals. A stylistic set strips the instructional arrows from the pictographs for a cleaner look. The single font file effectively delivers two visual weights through case differentiation rather than separate files.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Exercise as a workout-themed dingbat font featuring 26 exercise pictographs in two weights: light on lowercase a–z and bold on capitals A–Z. A stylistic set removes the instruction arrows from the figures. Released in 2023 and priced from $26.

name: BD Fluke
classification_notes: A bold, single-weight display typeface originally drawn for a concert poster, with no italic and a raw, utilitarian character typical of late-1990s club graphics.
summary: A bold display face originally drawn for a 1997 Fluke concert poster at Club Wasserwerk in Bern, released as a free font in 2012.
description: Created as a one-off piece of concert graphics in 1997, the typeface sat dormant for fifteen years before being released as a free font. It covers Basic Latin only, with a single Regular weight and no italic. Personal use is free; commercial projects require a licence.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Fluke as having been created for a concert poster for Fluke playing live at Club Wasserwerk in Bern in 1997. They released it as a free font in 2012, billing it as 'twenty two megatons bold' and making it available for personal use, with a paid licence required for commercial work.

name: BD Flossy
classification_notes: BD Flossy is a dingbat/picture font composed entirely of sheep characters rather than alphanumeric glyphs, placing it firmly in the display/ornamental dingbat category.
summary: A 1995 dingbat font from Büro Destruct built entirely from a single sheep character called Flossy, originally created as a Postscript Type 1 encoding experiment in Fontographer.
description: The first typeface released by Büro Destruct, BD Flossy encodes a sheep named Flossy across the character set in place of conventional letterforms. It originated as a technical experiment in encoding Postscript Type 1 fonts using Fontographer on Mac, at a time when the workflow was notoriously troublesome to configure correctly. Available as a free download, it is a curio of mid-1990s type experimentation rather than a workhorse text face.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Flossy as the first Büro Destruct font, created in 1995. The iconic BD sheep character Flossy was used as an experiment to encode Postscript Type 1 fonts in Fontographer on Mac, a process described as a nightmare until all settings were correctly configured. The result: sheep on green grass.

name: BD Fontabello
classification_notes: A lowercase-only revival inspired by a 1973 Mecanorma lettering sheet, with the rounded, characterful forms typical of early-70s display lettering used in record sleeve design.
summary: A single-weight revival of a 1973 Mecanorma display face, rediscovered on an Italian record sleeve and released in 2015 as a lowercase-only font.
description: Traced back to a font called Lucky from the Mecanorma catalogue, this typeface carries the rounded, warm character of early-1970s commercial lettering. It is lowercase only, a deliberate constraint that preserves the informal, label-ready feel of the source material. One weight, one style, suited to music, packaging and retro-flavoured editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Fontabello as inspired by a Mecanorma font called Lucky from 1973, discovered on an Italian record sleeve. Released in 2015, it is a lowercase-only typeface with a single Regular style. As the foundry put it: this font sounds good.

name: BD HexaDes
classification_notes: The font draws on the visual language of Akihabara's electronic signage, with geometric, angular letterforms that sit firmly in the techno or novelty display category rather than any conventional text type classification.
summary: A single-weight display typeface drawn from the angular electronic signage of Akihabara, originally released in 2001 and updated in 2024.
description: Conceived in 2001 and revised with broader language support in 2024, BD HexaDes takes its visual cues from the dense neon and LED lettering of Tokyo's Akihabara district. The single Regular weight features angular, geometric forms with a stylistic set offering alternate versions of key characters. Coverage spans over 25 Latin-based languages including Central and Eastern European scripts.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD HexaDes as inspired by the typefaces found in Akihabara, Tokyo, originally designed in 2001 with a wider language support and optimisation update in 2024.

name: BD Geminis
classification_notes: BD Geminis draws on 1970s and 1980s sci-fi and technology typography, giving it a strongly stylised, era-specific display character that resists classification as a functional text face.
summary: A single-weight display face modelled on the sci-fi and technology typography of the 1970s and 1980s, updated in 2024 with extended Latin coverage.
description: Originally released in 2003, BD Geminis interprets the geometric, angular letterforms associated with science fiction and consumer technology branding of the 1970s and 1980s. The 2024 update added full Latin Extended-A coverage and additional glyphs, broadening its usefulness across European languages. Available in one weight, it suits titling, packaging and themed projects where period-appropriate retrofuturism is the goal.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Geminis as an interpretation of several typeface variants used mainly in the 1970s and 1980s for sci-fi and technology related products, updated in 2024 with all Latin characters and additional glyphs.

name: BD Gravel
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with rounded and sharp-edged variants, designed with sport and outdoor themes in mind. The variable axis controlling corner rounding places it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with display-oriented character.
summary: A geometric sans built around an outdoor and cycling theme, available in Edge, Round and Rounder variants with a variable font axis controlling corner rounding.
description: Released in 2025, the family comes in three static styles — Edge, Round and Rounder — plus variable font versions that let the degree of corner rounding shift along a continuous axis. A wide set of stylistic alternates adds flexibility, and Latin Extended coverage spans 26 European languages. Suited to branding and editorial work in sport, travel and adventure contexts.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Gravel as a modern robust sans serif built for topics including biking, cycling, travelling, trekking, adventure and discovery. The family includes Edge, Round and Rounder styles, and the variable font allows the degree of rounding to be flexibly adjusted. Their tagline: Shifting Gears.

name: BD HitBit
classification_notes: A capitals-only display font inspired by a specific vintage computer logotype, placing it firmly in the retro/tech display category rather than any general-purpose classification.
summary: A capitals-only display font drawn from the Sony MSX HitBit computer logotype, suited to titling with a retro-tech character.
description: Directly referencing the Sony MSX HitBit logotype from the early home-computing era, this single-weight display font is intended for headline and titling use only. It covers a broad Latin character set supporting over twenty languages despite its all-caps construction. Best suited to projects requiring a period-specific technology aesthetic.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD HitBit as a display font inspired by the Sony MSX computers' HitBit logotype, designed for capital letters titling, released in 2009.

name: BD Karlo
classification_notes: A cartoony titler with variable baseline wiggle and optional distressing, firmly in the display category with comic-book vernacular aesthetics.
summary: A comic-inspired titler with three stylistic flavours and a variable font axis controlling baseline wiggle.
description: BD Karlo comes in clean straight, clean wiggly and subtly distressed wiggly variants, with the variable font allowing fine control over the degree of baseline undulation. Stylistic set alternates offer further character-level variation for capital letters. Language support covers a broad range of Latin-based European scripts.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Karlo as a groovy, cartoony comic titler released in 2024, available in three flavours: clean and straight, clean and wiggly, and subtle distressed and slightly wiggly. The variable font lets users fine-tune the wiggle of the baseline, and some capital letters include alternates to play with.

name: BD Jupiter
classification_notes: BD Jupiter draws from stencil lettering on architectural plans and military equipment, with open letterforms and serif details that place it firmly in the display category with a stencil subcategory.
summary: A stencil display family with serif details drawn from architectural plans and military markings, offered in regular and bold weights with matching stencil cuts.
description: Four styles make up the family: Regular, Bold, Regular Stencil and Bold Stencil. The letterforms carry open shapes with subtle serif details, drawing on the practical lettering found on instruction guides, technical plans and equipment crates. Suited to large-scale use on products, buildings, maps and printed titles where a utilitarian, constructional tone is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Jupiter as a font family inspired by stencil lettering on architectural plans, military boxes, machines and instruction guides. Its open shapes and serif details are extended further in the stencil variants, and it is intended for big lettering on products, buildings, maps, graphic charts, interface design and printed titles with a cool constructional approach.

name: BD Matrose
classification_notes: An uppercase-only display alphabet with a nautical aesthetic, designed for titling use only; the ornamental character and thematic styling place it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: An uppercase display alphabet from 2017 with a nautical theme, suited to marine-themed titles and headings.
description: A single-weight uppercase display face built around a nautical aesthetic, with OpenType ligatures including combinations for ff, fi, tt and their capitalised variants. Coverage spans Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and Latin Extended-A, making it serviceable across Western European languages. Best suited to short titling work where a period maritime character is the point.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Matrose as an uppercase display alphabet with nautical charm, inviting users to set their anchor and start typing marine titles.

name: BD Kickrom Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width, uppercase-oriented display face designed around retro computer terminal aesthetics, placing it firmly in the monospaced display category.
summary: A retro-futuristic monospaced display font built around an uppercase-primary character set with variants on capitals, aimed at headline and packaging use.
description: Released in 2016, BD Kickrom Mono draws on the visual language of early computer terminals and gaming hardware, using a fixed-width structure that suits tight headline settings. The main characters sit on the lowercase keys, with capital variants providing alternates. Its single Regular weight is best deployed on posters, game titles, music packaging and magazine headlines where a lo-fi machine aesthetic is required.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Kickrom Mono as a retro futuristic monospaced uppercase font suited to retro computer-related headlines in magazines, logotypes on posters, game titles, movies and music packaging.

name: BD LoFi
classification_notes: Originally drawn on an Amiga 1200 using TypeSmith in 1997, BD LoFi carries the characteristic low-resolution aesthetic of early pixel fonts, placing it firmly in the display/bitmap category.
summary: A pixel-style typeface originally drawn on an Amiga 1200 in 1997, updated in 2023 with expanded language support and a variable font version.
description: Born from an Amiga 1200 and TypeSmith software in 1997, BD LoFi carries the low-resolution character of late-1990s home computing. The 2023 revision adds a variable font spanning Regular to Bold, alongside extended Latin support covering over twenty European languages. It suits retro-themed digital projects, game interfaces and nostalgic editorial work where a lo-fi aesthetic is intentional.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD LoFi as a typeface originally designed on an Amiga 1200 with TypeSmith in 1997, updated in 2023 with wider language support and a variable version offering a bolder look.

name: BD MedLed
classification_notes: A monospaced LED dot-matrix display font built on a fixed 6x6 dot grid, placing it firmly in the mono category with a display character owing to its origin in electronic signage simulation.
summary: A monospaced LED dot-matrix font built on a 6x6 dot grid, originally created in 1995 for rave flyers and re-released with light refinements.
description: Built on a fixed 6x6 dot grid, this font mimics the appearance of LED display panels common in electronic signage and early digital interfaces. First drawn in 1995 and used across rave flyers of that era, the updated release adds minor refinements while retaining the original grid-based character. Available in Regular and Italic, with coverage limited to Basic Latin and a small selection of supplementary glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD MedLed as a monospaced LED display font drawn on a 6x6 dot grid, first created in 1995 and used on numerous rave flyers throughout the 1990s. This release brings a slightly refurbished and updated comeback to the original design.

name: BD MicronRobots
classification_notes: A variable dingbat font consisting entirely of pictorial micro-robot characters with no conventional letterforms; classified as display given its purely illustrative, non-typographic nature.
summary: A variable dingbat font containing 52 micro-robot characters that morph through five mutation stages via variable font axes.
description: Each of the 52 Micron characters is assigned to a standard Latin glyph slot, allowing the variable font's mutation axis to animate the robots through five distinct design states from their original form to a final mutated stage. The variable font technology drives the transformation between Muta1 and Muta5, making it functional as a motion or interactive design asset rather than a static set. Priced from $25 and released in 2019.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD MicronRobots as a variable dingbats font featuring 52 Microns, which are micro-tiny robot characters with five mutations. They note that variable font technology helped bring the Micron Robots to life, with Muta1 showing the robots in their original shape and Muta5 representing the final stage of their mutations.

name: BD MicronFont
classification_notes: Constructed from modular, geometric shapes evoking circuit boards and pixel grids; designed explicitly as a display typeface with a technological and playful aesthetic rather than for body text use.
summary: A modular, technology-inspired display typeface created for the TechnoCulture 2 festival in Fribourg in 2019, offered as a variable font across five width variants.
description: Originally commissioned as the visual identity typeface for TechnoCulture 2, a festival in Fribourg, Switzerland, BD MicronFont is built from geometric shapes that suggest circuitry and robotics. The variable font axis spans five named widths from Condensed to Extended, and the same glyph shapes were repurposed to construct a companion set of illustrated robot characters. Latin script coverage extends to Western and Northern European languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD MicronFont as a technologic yet playful typeface that breaks boundaries between technology, science, fiction and art. Originally the display face for TechnoCulture 2 in Fribourg, the font's modular glyph shapes were also used to build animated robot characters, with variable font technology helping to bring them to life.

name: BD Mother
classification_notes: Described as a cartoony slab serif with thin square inner shapes and curvy forms, placing it firmly in the slab category with a playful, display-oriented character.
summary: A single-weight cartoony slab serif from 2007, characterised by rounded curves and thin square counters suited to warm, informal messaging.
description: BD Mother pairs chunky slab construction with curvy, rounded letterforms and thin square inner shapes to produce a friendly, expressive character. Available in a single Regular weight, it covers a broad range of Latin-script languages including French, German, Spanish and Swedish. Best suited to packaging, greeting cards or any context calling for approachable, spoken-word energy.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Mother as a cartoony and curvy slab serif typeface with thin square inner shapes, suitable for friendly messages pronounced aloud.

name: BD Nippori
classification_notes: Inspired by the JR East railway logotype, BD Nippori has a strong geometric, signage-derived character typical of display typefaces drawn from transport and corporate logotype sources.
summary: A geometric display face drawn from the JR East railway logotype, named after a station on Tokyo's Yamanote Line.
description: Taking its cue from the abbreviated «JR» logotype of East Japan Railway Company, BD Nippori distils that clean, transport-signage geometry into a Latin display typeface. The family covers a broad range of Latin Extended characters across Western European languages, keeping the coverage practical for editorial and branding use. Its single style leans heavily on the source material's bold, legible simplicity.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Nippori as a font inspired by the East Japan Railway Company logotype abbreviation «JR», with the name taken from Nippori station on the Tokyo Yamanote loop line, a transfer point for several major lines including the Keihin-Tōhoku and Keisei Main Line.

name: BD Motra
classification_notes: An all-caps stencil face with wide, heavy letterforms intended for industrial signage and large-scale lettering applications. The stencil construction and uppercase-only primary design place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A fat, wide stencil uppercase face drawn from the lettering found on Honda's rare Motra CT50 off-road scooter, manufactured in Japan in 1982.
description: Designed in 2008 and rooted in a specific industrial artefact, BD Motra takes its letterforms from the stencil graphics on a 1982 Honda CT50 Motra scooter. The face is broad, heavy and uppercase-first, with alternate characters accessible on the lowercase keys. It suits large-scale applications: vehicle livery, cargo markings, building signage and product labelling where an unambiguous industrial register is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Motra as a fat, wide uppercase stencil font with character variants on the lowercase keys. It was inspired by the stencil lettering on the Honda Motra CT50, a rare Japanese off-road scooter from 1982, and is intended for big lettering on vehicles, cargo boxes, products and buildings with an industrial approach.

name: BD Panzer
classification_notes: A bold, uppercase liquid crystal display-style typeface with stylised alternate lowercase glyphs, squarely in the techno or LCD display genre used for titling and tech-themed work.
summary: An uppercase LCD-style display font from 2006, offering a regular and outlined variant with alternate lowercase glyphs suited to technology titlings.
description: Built around a bold liquid crystal aesthetic, the face is primarily uppercase with stylised alternate forms on the lowercase characters. Two styles are available: Regular and Outlined. Coverage spans basic Latin with extended accented characters for a range of Western European languages, keeping it practical for straightforward display use.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Panzer as a bold liquid crystal uppercase font with alternates on the lowercase characters, positioning it as particularly suited to technology-related titlings.

name: BD Orange
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a 'scripty grotesk', BD Orange is fundamentally a sans-serif grotesque with calligraphic influences and stylistic alternates rather than a true script typeface.
summary: A grotesque sans with calligraphic character, covering Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Japanese Katakana across five weights including a variable font.
description: BD Orange blends grotesque structure with scripty detailing, offering a large glyph set of 1025 characters alongside extensive OpenType alternates, discretionary ligatures and a higher x-height variant. Five static weights run from Thin to Black, complemented by a variable font, making it flexible across titling, logotype and body text settings. Its broad language coverage, including uppercase Cyrillic, Greek and Katakana, extends its usefulness across multilingual projects.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Orange as a picturesque scripty grotesk with a large number of alternates and discretionary ligatures, spanning delicate thin to loud black in a variable format. With 1025 glyphs it covers a wide range of languages including Cyrillic, Greek and Japanese Katakana, and they position it as a versatile choice for titling, logotypes and body text that balances classic and avant-garde qualities.

name: BD Obachan
classification_notes: An experimental uppercase Latin typeface with Asian calligraphic-inspired letterforms, placing it firmly in the novelty display category rather than any conventional classification.
summary: An experimental uppercase display typeface drawing on Asian calligraphic forms, updated and extended from the 2005 BD Mandarin.
description: Strictly uppercase, BD Obachan applies stylised strokes reminiscent of East Asian brushwork to Latin capitals, producing letterforms suited to packaging, poster design and branding that want a culturally inflected character. It is an update and expansion of BD Mandarin, originally designed in 2005, and ships in a single Regular weight. Latin Extended support covers a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Obachan as an experimental uppercase typeface with a distinctive Asian-inspired visual flair, balancing modern aesthetics with a nod to traditional Asian calligraphy. They position it for branding, packaging, posters and digital media, and note it as an updated, extended version of BD Mandarin from 2005.

name: BD Plakatbau
classification_notes: An all-caps geometric display face with a minimal character set, designed in a bold placard style consistent with constructivist and Swiss poster traditions.
summary: A minimal all-caps geometric display face from Büro Destruct, recognised from its use on several Radiohead album covers.
description: Released in 1995, this was one of the earliest typefaces from Büro Destruct and remains their most widely recognised, in part through its association with Radiohead's album artwork. The character set is extremely limited, covering basic Latin and a handful of German umlauts, making it a purely display proposition. Available as a free download for personal use, with a commercial licence available to purchase.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Plakatbau as one of the first BD alphabet free fonts and the Büro Destruct typeface with the smallest character set but the greatest popularity. They note its use by Radiohead on several album covers and offer it as a free download for personal projects, with a commercial licence available separately.

name: BD Pipe
classification_notes: A single-weight novelty geometric grotesque built around circular hole pictograms from Büro Destruct's pipe motif, making it a display typeface unsuitable for running text.
summary: A single-weight geometric grotesque from Büro Destruct's studio, built around the circular pipe-hole pictogram that appeared across several of their design works.
description: Released in 2006, BD Pipe takes a geometric grotesque skeleton and punches circular holes through its letterforms, referencing the pipe pictogram Büro Destruct used across multiple studio projects. It comes in one weight only and covers a broad range of Latin-based languages. Suited to display and branding contexts where a playful, studio-specific visual joke is the point.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Pipe as a funny circular holed geometric grotesk typeface, featuring the iconic Büro Destruct Pipe pictogram used across several of the studio's design works. They invoke smoke rings and describe it as Easy Peasy.

name: BD Radicons
classification_notes: BD Radicons is an icon font containing 110 pictographic glyphs mapped to Latin character slots, designed for UX and print use. It falls under the display/dingbats category as it contains no typographic letterforms.
summary: A 110-glyph icon font built on a two-line contrast system of bold and thin strokes, designed for UX, app and print contexts.
description: Originally developed as a 15-icon kit for an Adobe XD showcase at Awwwards Berlin in 2018, BD Radicons has since grown to 110 icons. Each glyph pairs a bold line with a thin line, a constraint that keeps the set visually consistent while requiring careful design balance. Purchasers receive an Adobe XD template with all icons pre-saved as elements.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Radicons as a versatile icon font of 110 glyphs built on a radical minimalist principle: two strongly contrasting lines, one bold and one thin. First shown as a 15-icon free kit at Adobe Awwwards Berlin in 2018, the set has expanded to cover UX design on websites, in apps and for print.

name: BD Radiogram
classification_notes: A decorative thin-line typeface with curvy letterforms in Round and Narrow styles, placing it firmly in the display category with no practical body-text application.
summary: A thin-line display typeface from 2007 offered in two styles: a curved Round cut and a tighter Narrow cut.
description: Both styles share a delicate, curvy construction built on consistently thin strokes, giving the family an elegant, almost art-deco inflection. The Round cut favours soft, open forms while the Narrow cut tightens the proportions for headlines and logotype work at closer measures. Latin Extended coverage is included, broadening its usefulness across Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Radiogram as an elegant, curvy, thin-line typeface released in 2007, available in two styles: Round and Narrow.

name: BD RatterBit
classification_notes: A pixelated cut-out display typeface with a deliberately rugged, retro aesthetic rooted in bitmap grid construction, placing it firmly in the display/pixel category.
summary: A pixelated cut-out display typeface first released in 1995, expanded in 2024 with full uppercase, lowercase and broad Latin language support.
description: Originally from 1995 and revisited in 2024, this single-weight display face combines pixelated construction with a cut-out aesthetic that sits somewhere between retro gaming typography and graffiti-influenced design. The 2024 update brought lowercase characters, extended symbols and coverage across more than two dozen European languages. Best suited to gaming interfaces, digital art and editorial contexts where an unpolished, high-energy texture is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD RatterBit as a bold, rugged, pixelated cut-out typeface that blends retro and funk influences, positioning it as ideal for gaming, digital art and edgy design projects. The 2024 extended version adds uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols and wide language support to make it more versatile.

name: BD Relaunch
classification_notes: A stylised display face combining Latin Roman letterforms with Japanese Katakana glyphs, occupying a niche between Latin display and mixed-script novelty type.
summary: A single-weight display face from 2000, relaunched in 2024, pairing Latin Roman letterforms with Japanese Katakana glyphs contributed by Savwo of Cyclone Graphix in Tokyo.
description: Originally created in 2000 and formally relaunched in 2024, this single-weight face covers Basic Latin, West European accented characters and a full Katakana block. The Katakana glyphs were drawn by Savwo from Cyclone Graphix in Tokyo, giving the Latin-Katakana combination a cross-cultural graphic energy typical of early 2000s design. It suits display and headline use where a Y2K-inflected, Japan-adjacent aesthetic is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Relaunch as a 2024 relaunch of a font originally created in 2000, featuring Japanese Katakana glyphs designed by Savwo from Cyclone Graphix in Tokyo, with support for Basic Latin, West European languages and Japanese Katakana.

name: BD Retrocentric
classification_notes: A display typeface drawing on 1960s and 70s logotype aesthetics, blending retro and futurist influences into a single eccentric style. Its single weight and decorative character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight display typeface drawing on 1960s and 70s logotype conventions, blending retro form with a faintly futurist edge.
description: Inspired by the corporate logotypes of the sixties and seventies, BD Retrocentric occupies the space between retro nostalgia and period-specific futurism. Available in one weight with broad Latin language support across more than twenty languages, it is aimed at identities and headline work that wants to evoke the typographic confidence of large traditional companies from that era. The character set covers standard Latin extended glyphs including diacritics for Western and Northern European languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Retrocentric as a typeface inspired by logotypes from the sixties and seventies, characterised by a balanced blend of retro and futurism. They position it as the typographic language of large, tradition-conscious companies, summing up its spirit with the phrase "think retro eccentric".

name: BD Roylac
classification_notes: A display typeface with a distinctive split-form construction where a thin curving line divides filled shapes, rooted in 1970s lowercase drawings by Jacques Loison. Its expressive, retro-futuristic character places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight display typeface built around a thin curving line that bisects filled letterforms, drawing on lowercase glyphs originally sketched by Jacques Loison in 1972.
description: Each glyph in BD Roylac is defined by a thin, curvy line that splits two filled shapes, sometimes tracing a readable path and sometimes cutting across it for decorative effect. The design revives lowercase characters drawn by Jacques Loison in 1972, with a number of these original forms available through stylistic alternates. Released in 2021, the typeface was created as a tribute to Eric de Broche des Combes on his fiftieth birthday.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Roylac as rooted in lowercase glyphs drawn by Jacques Loison in 1972, several of which are preserved as stylistic alternates. They file it under retro-futuristic design, characterised by a thin, curvy line that divides two filled shapes within each character, sometimes aiding legibility and sometimes working against it. The font was dedicated to BD fanboy Monsieur Eric de Broche des Combes to mark his fiftieth anniversary.

name: BD Ritmic
classification_notes: A techno-inflected display face with electronic music origins, designed for logotype work and expressive headline use rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight display face born from a Drum'n'Bass label logotype in 2002, updated in 2023 with broader language support and capital letters.
description: Originally drawn for a Drum'n'Bass label in 2002, the design carries the hard-edged energy of electronic music graphics into type. A 2023 update added capital letters, extended Latin language support across nearly 30 languages, and general refinements. Available as a single Regular weight, it is built for headline and logotype contexts where visual impact outweighs versatility.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Ritmic as originating from a Drum'n'Bass label logotype designed in 2002. In 2023 the font was updated with wider language support, capital letters and other optimisations. Their own words: "BD Ritmic sounds loud and drums the big beat."

name: BD Spinner
classification_notes: A display font derived from logotypes and icons created for the Blade Runner film production, featuring a distinctive retro-futuristic aesthetic rather than any conventional text category.
summary: A free Blade Runner tribute font built from original production logotypes and icons drawn by Tom Southwell and digitised by Büro Destruct in 2004 and 2023.
description: Originally drawn by Tom Southwell, production illustrator on Ridley Scott's 1982 film, the glyphs were carefully vectorised by Büro Destruct across two rounds of work in 2004 and 2023. Coverage spans Basic Latin and a set of dingbats, with an alternate numeral set available via stylistic set. Available for personal use only.
foundry_blurb: BD Spinner is described as a free Blade Runner tribute font featuring logotypes and icons created by Tom Southwell, production illustrator for Ridley Scott's 1982 film, carefully vectorised by Büro Destruct in 2004 and 2023, and available for personal use only.

name: BD Supper
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as geometric-organic, the letterforms blend circular geometric construction with soft, rounded organic details giving it a cartoonish, playful character distinct from neutral geometric grotesques.
summary: A two-weight geometric-organic sans with a rounded, cartoonish character suited to food packaging and gastronomy branding.
description: Built on geometric foundations softened with organic curves, it occupies a cheerful register somewhere between children's illustration and contemporary FMCG packaging. Two weights, Regular and Bold, cover basic hierarchy needs from body text to headlines, and three stylistic sets plus a wide set of alternate characters give designers room to adjust the personality. The glyph set covers a broad range of Latin-based European languages.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Supper as a friendly and humorous geometric-organic sans serif made for food packaging and gastronomy topics. It has a modern, cartoonish and childlike feel while remaining clean and bold, and offers a wide selection of alternate characters alongside two weights for structuring body text with headlines and creating logotypes.

name: BD St.Moritz
classification_notes: Inspired by Swiss tourism poster typography from the 1960s, the typeface has a period-specific display character that places it firmly in the display category with a retro or vintage subcategory rather than a neutral grotesque.
summary: A single-weight display face drawn from Swiss tourism poster lettering of the 1960s, originally released in 2003 and revised in 2023.
description: The design takes direct reference from the hand-lettered and typeset titling found on mid-century Swiss tourist posters, giving it a warm, period character suited to retro branding and editorial work. Only one weight exists, limiting versatility, though the 2023 update improved glyph coverage and kerning. Latin Extended coverage supports a broad spread of Western European languages including Scandinavian and Iberian variants.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD St.Moritz as inspired by the typography found on Swiss tourism posters from the sixties. Originally designed in 2003, it received an update in 2023 adding more glyphs and improved kerning.

name: BD Tatami
classification_notes: A blocky, modular typeface with angled edges inspired by Japanese woodcraft. The geometric, experimental construction and overt decorative intent place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A modular display typeface from 2001 inspired by Japanese woodcraft, built from blocky letterforms with slightly angled edges.
description: Drawn during a prolonged stay in Japan, BD Tatami takes its cues from traditional woodcraft joinery, rendering Latin letters as interlocking rectangular modules with subtly angled edges. The family consists of a single Regular weight covering very basic Latin, with readability deliberately secondary to its decorative, experimental character. Best suited to short-form display settings where its structural rhythm can be appreciated at large sizes.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Tatami as a typeface inspired by Japanese woodcraft, conceived during an extended stay in Japan. Its defining trait is a blocky appearance with slightly angled edges, and the foundry explicitly note that readability is secondary, positioning it as a font for experimentation with modular shapes.

name: BD Telegraph
classification_notes: A geometric-leaning grotesque sans designed for titling and short text, with variable axes covering width and weight. The condensed and expanded variants, alongside the sporty character alternates, align it with a display-oriented grotesque tradition.
summary: A variable sans with width and weight axes, originally designed in 2011 and updated in 2023, aimed at sports, gaming and screen entertainment contexts.
description: Spanning Light to Medium across Condensed, Normal and Expanded widths, the family is available as a variable font covering both axes simultaneously. Two stylistic sets offer alternate characters for additional personality, and the extended Latin coverage supports a broad range of European languages. It suits titling and short-form text in sports graphics, film titles and game interfaces rather than extended body copy.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Telegraph as an elegant, sportive variable typeface with two axes controlling width and weight. They note its flexibility makes it suitable for titling and shorter body texts in sports, games, science, and film and TV contexts, with a wide selection of alternate characters in stylistic sets and extended Latin language support.

name: BD Terminal
classification_notes: Constructed from a grid of dots to simulate a dot-matrix terminal aesthetic, intended for display use across posters, UI and experimental layouts rather than continuous text setting.
summary: A variable dot-matrix display typeface with two axes spanning weight and width, covering Latin Extended, Greek, Cyrillic and Japanese Katakana.
description: Built from a blotch-dot grid that evokes terminal and dot-matrix displays, the family ships as both static styles and a variable font with weight and width axes. The static cuts run across Light, Regular and Bold in standard and extended widths, while the variable version allows continuous interpolation between the two. Script coverage is broad for a display face, taking in Latin Extended, Greek, Cyrillic and Katakana for interface labels and experimental multilingual layouts.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Terminal VF as a hyper-flexible terminal typeface that turns any layout into a living dot-matrix display. Its dot construction morphs smoothly from tight, light micro-typographic detail to wide, bold billboard headlines via two variable axes, moving between precise technical UI copy and playful poster work. The family supports Latin Extended, Greek, Cyrillic and Japanese Katakana for use across interfaces, labels and experimental multilingual layouts.

name: BD Tinyfont
classification_notes: A pixel typeface built on a 3x3 grid, placing it firmly in the display category as a bitmap/pixel subcategory. Its extreme grid constraint makes it unsuitable for body text and characteristic of novelty display use.
summary: A pixel typeface constructed on a 3x3 grid, originally designed for Büro Destruct in 2005 and updated with a slightly extended glyph set in 2023.
description: Built on the smallest practical pixel grid, it pushes legibility to its absolute limit while remaining functional for short-form display text. Originally conceived by Bataais for Büro Destruct, the file weighs in at just six kilobytes. Free for personal use, with a commercial licence available for professional projects.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Tinyfont as a pixel typeface constructed on a minimal 3x3 pixel grid, originally conceptualised by Bataais in 2005 for Büro Destruct, with a slightly extended glyph set updated in 2023. The font is free for personal projects, with a licence required for commercial use.

name: BD Varicolor
classification_notes: A heavy-weight stencil display typeface drawing on early-20th-century architectural and Art Deco letterforms, intended solely for titling use rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight stencil display face rooted in early-20th-century Art Deco and architectural lettering, suited to titling in art and design publications.
description: Drawing on the geometric weight and structural quality of Art Deco and early modernist architectural lettering, BD Varicolor offers a heavy stencil character designed for large-scale titling use. The family ships as a single Regular weight covering Western Latin script across ten languages. Its application is narrow but deliberate: posters, magazine headlines and art-adjacent editorial contexts where the period aesthetic is the point.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Varicolor as a new take on typefaces and shapes from the early-20th-century architectural and Art Deco style, noting that its heavy weighted stencil character works well for titling in art-related publications.

name: BD Unicorse
classification_notes: A mixed-case display face with a distinctly retro-futuristic character, designed for headline and packaging use rather than extended reading. The unconventional case assignment and stylistic ligatures place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight mixed-case display font from 2010 with a retro-futuristic character, featuring 12 ligatures and alternate glyphs assigned to capital keys.
description: The font places its main character set on lowercase keys and alternative forms on capitals, creating an unusual mixed-case typographic register suited to headlines and logotypes. Twelve ligatures extend its compositional options for poster and packaging work. Its angular, retro-futuristic styling points towards games, film and music contexts.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Unicorse as a retro futuristic mixed case display font, with main characters on the small keys and alternatives on the capitals. They cite 12 ligatures and recommend it for headlines in magazines, logotypes, posters, games, movies and music packaging.

name: BD Victoria
classification_notes: A single-weight grotesque sans drawing directly from the London Underground's station information panel lettering, with the blocky uppercase forms and signage conventions typical of transport grotesques.
summary: A grotesque sans drawn from the uppercase lettering used on London Underground station information panels, first released in 2004.
description: Originating in 2004 as a revival of the London Underground's station panel lettering, BD Victoria captures the blunt, institutional character of transport signage grotesques. It ships in a single Regular weight with Latin Extended coverage across 23 languages, updated in 2023 with improved kerning and broader language support. Best suited to display and wayfinding contexts where that underground-adjacent tone is the point.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Victoria as inspired by the uppercase font used on London Underground station information panels, originally designed in 2004. In 2023 the typeface was updated with wider language support and improved kerning. They sign off with the line: London calling!

name: BD Vectrons
classification_notes: An 8-bit style pixel font designed to evoke retro video game aesthetics, placing it firmly in the display category with a pixel or bitmap subcategory.
summary: An 8-bit retro pixel font in three stylistic variants — Regular, Linescan and Rounded — designed for mixing and matching to create an authentic lo-fi game aesthetic.
description: Released in 2021, it draws on the visual language of classic video game displays, offering three distinct cuts that work best in combination. Alternate uppercase glyphs and the contrasting styles of Linescan and Rounded give designers room to vary the texture of a layout. Coverage is limited to Basic Latin and a modest set of supplementary characters, so it suits display and titling use rather than long-form text.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Vectrons as an 8-bit style retro game pixel font coming in three flavours — Regular, Linescan and Rounded — and recommend mixing the different uppercase glyphs and styles together for best results.

name: BD Viewmaster
classification_notes: An uppercase-only display typeface inspired by vintage View-Master product lettering, with a decorative neon variant that places it firmly in the display category with a retro aesthetic.
summary: An uppercase display typeface drawn from the lettering of the iconic View-Master stereoscope toy, offered in three styles including a neon variant.
description: Modelled on the lettering found on View-Master products, this is an uppercase-only Latin family released in 2008 in Light, Regular and Neon styles. The neon cut gives the design a glowing, illuminated quality suited to retro-themed signage or packaging. Language support covers a broad range of Western European Latin scripts.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Viewmaster as a font inspired by the typeface used on the legendary View-Master products, available in three styles: Light, Regular and Neon.

name: BD Westwork
classification_notes: A very condensed bold pixel typeface designed explicitly for display use, with stylistic alternates and a bitmap-inspired aesthetic drawn from pixel grid construction.
summary: A very condensed bold pixel display typeface with stylistic alternates, inspired by the 1973 Westworld film.
description: Built on a pixel grid and drawn in a single very condensed bold style, BD Westwork is intended for display and headline use. Stylistic alternates for letters including B, C, D, F, L, M, P, R, W, X, Y and Z allow for visual variation within a single weight. The foundry suggests it works well in combination with delicate illustrations, photography and serif or sans-serif body text.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Westwork as a very condensed, bold and distinctive pixel display typeface with stylistic alternates, noting it works great in combination and in contrast with delicate illustrations, photos and body text with and without serifs. Released in 2018, it is inspired by the 1973 American science fiction western film Westworld.

name: BD Weakon
classification_notes: A pixel typeface recreated from an early 1980s Atari game, making it a display-oriented bitmap design with a retro gaming aesthetic.
summary: A pixel typeface recreated and extended from the Atari 400/800 game Weakon, originally programmed by Eric Freeman in 1983.
description: Built from the bitmap letterforms of a 1983 Atari game, BD Weakon captures the chunky, lo-fi character of early home computing. The single Regular weight covers Basic Latin and West European characters, making it suitable for retro gaming projects, display headings and nostalgia-driven work. Released in 2024 by Type Different.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Weakon as a recreation and extension of a basic typeface from the Atari 400/800 game Weakon, programmed by Eric Freeman in 1983, characterised as funky and fun pixels.

name: BD Wakarimasen
classification_notes: A display typeface covering only Japanese Hiragana and Katakana scripts, combining two contrasting styles (formerly BD Hiragana Kuro and BD Wakarimasu) for use in Japanese-language typesetting. No Latin character support is included.
summary: A Japanese-only typeface combining two contrasting kana styles, Hiragana and Katakana, with no Latin character support whatsoever.
description: Originally released in 2011 as two separate fonts (BD Hiragana Kuro and BD Wakarimasu), the family was consolidated under the BD Wakarimasen name. The two kana styles are designed to create visual contrast when mixed in Japanese text, giving designers a built-in typographic tension between Hiragana and Katakana passages. Basic punctuation and CJK symbols are included alongside a stylistic set for alternate punctuation forms.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BD Wakarimasen as combining their Japanese Hiragana font (formerly BD Hiragana Kuro) and Japanese Katakana font (formerly BD Wakarimasu) into a single family. The two styles are intended to create strong contrast when used together in Japanese texts, and the font contains no Latin letters.

name: Edding850
classification_notes: Designed to replicate the visual character of writing with an edding 850 marker pen, placing it firmly in the display/script-adjacent category of hand-drawn marker typefaces.
summary: A single-weight marker typeface commissioned by Edding in 2011, replicating the stroke character of the edding 850 felt-tip pen.
description: Commissioned by Edding Germany and released in 2011, this typeface captures the thick, uniform strokes of the edding 850 marker pen. It was originally deployed as a webfont for an interactive write-board called Type-for-Type, where visitors could compose and download their own text. Free for personal use, with a commercial licence available.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe Edding850 as a typeface commissioned by Edding Germany to express the distinctive style of writing with the edding 850 marker pen on screen. It was used on the interactive Type-for-Type write-board, and a short documentary was made about the studio's process of creating it.

name: BDR A3MiK
classification_notes: An all-caps constructivist display face with rigid, angular geometry and a futuristic aesthetic, designed specifically for titling rather than body text.
summary: An all-caps geometric display face from 2009 with a hard, constructivist structure suited to science fiction titling, covering Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Japanese Katakana.
description: Built around rigid, angular letterforms, this single-weight display face is aimed squarely at titling in science fiction contexts. The glyph set is unusually broad for a niche display font, extending beyond Latin Extended to include Russian Cyrillic, Greek and Japanese Katakana. It offers no italic or weight variants, making it a specialist tool rather than a workhorse.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BDR A3MiK as an uppercase alphabet with alternatives including European, Eastern European, Russian Cyrillic, Greek and Japanese Katakana characters, noting its strong, constructive and futuristic appearance as well suited to titling in science fiction related media.

name: BDR Mono 2021
classification_notes: An uppercase monospaced typeface originally from 1999, now offered in Normal and Rounded subfamilies. Its fixed-width, geometric construction places it squarely in the mono category.
summary: An all-caps monospaced family from Büro Destruct's Lopetz, updated in 2021 with a rounded subfamily and full Western and Central European character support.
description: Originally a single-weight Mac PostScript Type 1 font from 1999, BDR Mono grew to include Light, Regular and Bold weights in 2006 before this 2021 revision added a parallel Rounded subfamily. The six-style family covers broad Latin coverage for European languages from Icelandic to Turkish. Its fixed-width, uppercase geometry suits display, signage and screen work with a functional Swiss edge.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe BDR Mono 2021 as Büro Destruct's monospaced uppercase typeface with a long history in their library. First designed by Lopetz in 1999 as a single-weight PostScript font, it gained a Light, Regular and Bold family in 2006 before receiving a 2021 update that added a Rounded subfamily and a complete glyph set covering languages across Europe.

name: NaraNino
classification_notes: Digitised directly from the handwriting of a six-year-old child, giving it an authentic, uneven, casual hand quality typical of naïve or children's handwriting fonts.
summary: A single-weight font drawn directly from the handwriting of designer Lopetz's six-year-old son, with royalties going to the child's piggybank.
description: Digitised from real childhood handwriting in 2012, the font carries the natural irregularity of a young hand still learning the alphabet. Coverage is basic Latin with Latin-1 supplements and a small selection of extended characters. It suits projects that call for an authentic, unaffected children's hand rather than a polished calligraphic imitation.
foundry_blurb: Type Different describe NaraNino as a faithful capture of Lopetz's son Nara Nino's handwriting at the age of six, at which point he had learned every letter of the alphabet. Royalties from sales go directly to the boy's piggybank.
